content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
107 SOUTH BROADWAY • • • SUITE 7007 • • • LOS ANGELES, 90012

March 12, 1979

Ms. Stella Wallace
Registrar-Recorder of
Los Angeles County
Campaign Reporting Unit
808 North Spring Street
Los Angeles, California 90012

Dear Ms. Wallace:

This letter is to confirm our telephone conversation of
March 7, 1979.

The question you posed was whether or not a penalty that
has been assessed a committee included the date of the postmark
of that campaign statement that was mailed, or the date that your
office actually received the campaign statement?

I advised that the penalty should include and end on the
date of the postmark of the mailed campaign statement. The
same principal that is used for campaign statements which
are mailed must be postmarked by no later than midnight of
the date on which the campaign statement is due is
applicable. This advice has been confirmed by Barbara Campbell
of our Legal Division in Sacramento. I have enclosed a copy
of a factsheet which the Commission has developed for the
filing of campaign statements by mail (Government Code
Section 81007) for your information.

If our office can be of further assistance, please
do not hesitate to call.

Sincerely,

Helen P. Andino
Special Compliance
Representative

HPA:jg

Enclosure: Factsheet 7/78

SACRAMENTO (916) 322-5660 • LOS ANGELES (213) 620-5196

sections.question: whether or not a penalty that has been assessed a committee included the date of the postmark of that campaign statement that was mailed, or the date that your office actually received the campaign statement?
sections.conclusion: I advised that the penalty should include and end on the date of the postmark of the mailed campaign statement. The same principal that is used for campaign statements which are mailed must be postmarked by no later than midnight of the date on which the campaign statement is due is applicable.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a penalty assessment date for a late campaign statement use the postmark date or the date when the local registrar actually receives the statement?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that penalties should be assessed based on the postmark date of the mailed campaign statement, applying the same principle used for determining whether a statement is timely filed.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a follow-up confirmation letter from a telephone conversation. Question and conclusion sections are embedded in body text rather than formally labeled. Letter references Government Code Section 81007 and was confirmed by FPPC Legal Division. Handwritten notation at bottom indicates "t 7/78" (possibly a file date reference).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: whether or not a penalty that has been assessed a committee included the date of the postmark of that campaign statement that was mailed, or the date that your office actually received the campaign statement?

CONCLUSION: I advised that the penalty should include and end on the date of the postmark of the mailed campaign statement. The same principal that is used for campaign statements which are mailed must be postmarked by no later than midnight of the date on which the campaign statement is due is applicable.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to Los Angeles County Registrar-Recorder regarding whether penalty assessments for late campaign statements should use the postmark date or receipt date, confirming that the postmark date controls.
